text: Hello, I'm sharing a sigmoid colectomy for diverticulitis. This patient was in the hospital a few weeks earlier with a very small perforation in the pelvis, a two centimeter abscess that went away with antibiotics. Had had numerous attacks of diverticulitis and therefore was recommended for interval resection. patient was completely asymptomatic prior to coming in for surgery.
The surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection approach, initially working in an unviolated plane to facilitate the subsequent lateral dissection.
The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection to mobilize the bowel, which is adhered to the pelvic side walls, bladder, and uterus.
The surgeon visually searches for the ureter, briefly glimpsing it. The use of ICG (indocyanine green) is noted for its effectiveness in quickly identifying the ureter.
The surgeon identifies the ureter after dissecting laterally, but has not yet taken the mesentery.
The surgeon protects the retroperitoneum and proceeds with lateral dissection by the descending colon, which is visibly lateralized, facilitating an easier dissection process.

The surgeon attempts to connect the previously medially dissected plane with the lateral dissection near the left ureter, without needing to mobilize the splenic flexure due to the elongated descending and sigmoid mesentery.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and begins the left pelvic dissection, aiming to connect the medial and lateral dissection planes. A surgical sponge is visible, and the use of ICG mode enhances visualization of the ureter, ensuring safer dissection by providing a clear view of posterior structures.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection near the left posterior dome of the bladder using a robotic suction instrument, navigating the complex anatomical structures visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon encounters difficulty finding dissection planes as they navigate adherent tissue around the colon. The dissection progresses into retroperitoneal fat, with the surgeon suspecting proximity to the left tube but without identifying the uterus. The right mesorectum and adnexa appear drawn into the surgical field, complicating the effort to access the deep pelvis.

The surgeon navigates between the posterior and lateral total mesorectal excision (TME) planes, attempting to establish a clear dissection path while moving from known to unknown anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon examines a thickened area believed to be where the abscess was located, noting the dense tissue consistency.

The surgeon continues to navigate the challenging dissection by moving back and forth between planes. The decision is made to take the right adnexa to improve exposure and mobility in the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts anterior dissection but, unable to identify the uterus, switches to performing posterior dissection.
The surgeon manipulates the bowel by pulling it left, right, anteriorly, and posteriorly to increase mobility. The dissection follows a visible plane up and around laterally.

The surgeon identifies the residual abscess cavity, which has transformed into a pseudo diverticulum within the bowel. The surgeon carefully dissects outside of this area, frequently checking ICG imaging to ensure the ureter is not involved in the tissue being cauterized.

The surgeon encounters gonadal bleeding and cleans the camera lens. They proceed to detach the remaining attachments of the pseudo diverticulum, noting additional bleeding from the mucosal edge of the bowel. The left gonadal vessels are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the complex of the left gonadal vessels being drawn into the perforated area. An anterior plane is developed to better visualize the anatomy, revealing a structure suspected to be the uterus. The presence of a large exophytic uterine fibroid is confirmed, aiding in anatomical orientation. The left adnexa is seen drawn into the perforated segment of the bowel.

The surgeon is dissecting in the anterior cul-de-sac, working to separate a large uterine fibroid from the bowel. The focus is on identifying the soft, pliable rectum and addressing the last portion of the left adnexa.
The surgeon identifies the left lateral mesorectum and suspends the fibroid uterus to improve exposure, as there is no assist port available for this case.

The surgeon resumes dissection, addressing right anterior adhesions while carefully avoiding injury to the vagina. The dissection follows the previously developed total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, facilitating anterior progress.
The surgeon identifies the cul-de-sac, known as the Pouch of Douglas, and develops the area to reveal the anterior fat, aiming to locate the healthy rectum beneath.

The surgeon clears the thickened mesorectum, located slightly above the previously delineated area at the level of the peritoneal reflection. Using a vessel sealer, multiple firings are performed to address the tissue, with some failures observed but managed effectively.

The surgeon takes the proximal rectum, having already dissected the superior hemorrhoidal artery distal to the takeoff of the left colic artery. The mesentery in this area appears tremendously thickened, and the surgeon approaches the proximal margin, selecting a section visibly devoid of gross diverticulum.

The surgeon clears the mesentery before administering ICG to avoid false positives in assessing blood supply. Under ICG mode, the surgeon marks the margins and begins distal transection, preparing for a sewn anastomosis due to the bowel's thickness being unsuitable for staplers.
The surgeon identifies a diverticulum laterally, initially missed during the procedure. The proximal bowel is transected, revealing a wide open and healthy margin without any visible diverticulum.
The surgeon encounters a diverticulum during the procedure, excises it, and places it inside the patient for later expulsion. The specimen is then moved to the right upper quadrant.

The surgeon examines the presumed rectal stump, noting a narrow lumen with diverticulum extending several centimeters into what was thought to be the rectal lumen. The presence of residual sigmoid tissue is identified, which must be removed as it is unsuitable for anastomosis.

The surgeon identifies and transects the original diverticulum across the bowel, ensuring no residual sigmoid tissue remains for the anastomosis. This careful examination prevents potential anastomosis failures due to leftover sigmoid tissue.

rectal stump, I think that a lot of recurrences of diverticulitis can be because of leaving tissue that is at risk for recurrence on the distal side, not the proximal side. Clearly the more high pressure side, you saw how narrow that lumen was where those ticks were. And so taking this off was very important, even though this case took a long time and this was frustrating. You just got to regroup and you got to do it.
The surgeon transects the specimen using a vessel sealer, addressing the remaining lateral mesentery. The small specimen is extracted transanally.

The surgeon begins a single-layer hand-sewn anastomosis, starting with a posterior suture using trio silk to invert the posterior lip. Large bites are taken to ensure alignment, with both sides of the anastomosis showing satisfactory caliber.

The surgeon checks the ICG fluorescence to ensure adequate perfusion before proceeding. The upper part of the tissue is removed, and the surgeon begins placing the posterior suture for the anastomosis.

The surgeon takes large bites with the suture and turns to start the canal over the right anterior aspect. Upon inspection, a residual diverticulum is identified on the distal side, which is then excised.

The surgeon returns to the anterior sutures, carefully aligning them in the midline for joining and tying. The sutures are adjusted to maintain equal distance on both the proximal and distal margins, with larger bites taken on the sigmoid side and smaller bites on the right side.

The surgeon inverts the mucosa as the anastomosis comes together, tying the sutures securely. A lead test is performed with fluid placed posteriorly to remove trapped air bubbles. A Blake drain is left in the pelvis.
